subject: ap-psychology

core_concepts.title: Theories of Motivation
core_concepts.summary: Drive-reduction theory (Clark Hull) proposes that physiological needs create drives (tension states) motivating behavior to restore homeostasis. Arousal theory (Yerkes-Dodson law) proposes an inverted-U relationship — performance peaks at moderate arousal, with complex tasks requiring lower optimal arousal than simple tasks. Maslow's hierarchy of needs arranges needs from physiological (food, water) → safety → love/belonging → esteem → self-actualization — lower needs must be met before higher needs dominate. Incentive theory emphasizes external stimuli (rewards, goals) that pull behavior, contrasting with drive theory's internal push. Intrinsic motivation (internal interest) vs. extrinsic motivation (external reward) — the overjustification effect shows external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation.

core_concepts.title: Hunger, Sex, and Other Motivations
core_concepts.summary: The hypothalamus is the hunger regulation center — the lateral hypothalamus stimulates hunger; the ventromedial hypothalamus signals satiety. Set-point theory proposes a biologically regulated target weight the body defends through hunger and metabolism adjustments. Leptin (from fat cells) signals satiety to the brain. External factors (food cues, portion size, social eating) also drive eating. Sexual motivation is shaped by biological factors (hormones) but is heavily influenced by psychological factors (fantasy, cultural norms, relationship context) — unlike hunger, it can be aroused without physiological deprivation. Sensation seeking is a trait reflecting desire for novel, intense experiences — more frequent in adolescence due to incomplete prefrontal cortex development.

core_concepts.title: Theories of Emotion
core_concepts.summary: James-Lange theory: stimulus → physiological arousal → awareness of arousal → emotion (we feel afraid because we notice we are trembling). Cannon-Bard theory: thalamus simultaneously triggers physiological response AND emotional experience — arousal and emotion are parallel, not sequential. Schachter-Singer two-factor theory: physiological arousal + cognitive label → emotion. The same arousal can become different emotions depending on context (Schachter and Singer's epinephrine injection study). Lazarus's cognitive-appraisal theory: cognitive appraisal of a situation determines the emotional response. Paul Ekman identified six universal basic emotions (happiness, sadness, anger, fear, disgust, surprise) with cross-culturally consistent facial expressions — supporting a biological basis. The facial feedback hypothesis proposes that making a facial expression influences emotional experience.

core_concepts.title: Psychoanalytic Theory of Personality
core_concepts.summary: Freud proposed three levels of consciousness (conscious, preconscious, unconscious) and three personality structures: id (pleasure principle, entirely unconscious), ego (reality principle, largely conscious), and superego (morality principle). Defense mechanisms protect the ego from anxiety: repression (unconsciously blocking distressing thoughts), rationalization (self-justifying logic), projection (attributing own feelings to others), displacement (redirecting impulses to safer target), reaction formation (behaving opposite to true feelings), sublimation (channeling into valued activities). Psychosexual stages: oral (0-18m), anal (18m-3y), phallic (3-6y, Oedipus/Electra complex), latency (6-puberty), genital (puberty+). Fixation at a stage produces related adult traits — oral fixation: dependence; anal fixation: rigidity or messiness.

core_concepts.title: Humanistic, Social-Cognitive, and Trait Theories
core_concepts.summary: Humanistic theories (Maslow, Rogers) emphasize positive human nature, conscious experience, and growth toward self-actualization. Rogers: psychological health requires congruence between actual self and ideal self; unconditional positive regard promotes growth vs. conditions of worth that distort self-concept. Social-cognitive theory (Bandura): personality emerges from reciprocal determinism — mutual interaction of personal factors (cognition), behavior, and environment. Self-efficacy — belief in one's ability to succeed — shapes motivation and achievement. Big Five trait theory (OCEAN): Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism — empirically derived, cross-culturally stable personality dimensions. Conscientiousness is the strongest Big Five predictor of academic and job success. The MMPI is an objective personality inventory; the Rorschach and TAT are projective tests (criticized for low reliability and validity).

key_terms.definition: Behavior is motivated by the need to reduce tension created by unmet biological needs (Hull)
Tendency of the body to maintain a stable internal state
Performance peaks at moderate arousal — too little or too much arousal impairs performance
Pyramid of needs from physiological → safety → love/belonging → esteem → self-actualization
Maslow's highest need — fulfilling one's potential and becoming all one is capable of being
Motivation driven by internal interest, enjoyment, or satisfaction
Motivation driven by external rewards, grades, money, or approval
External rewards undermine intrinsic motivation by shifting attribution from interest to reward
Each person has a biologically regulated target weight the body defends through hunger and metabolism
Emotion is the awareness of physiological arousal: arousal → perceived emotion
Thalamus simultaneously triggers both physiological arousal and conscious emotion
Schachter-Singer: emotion = physiological arousal + cognitive label applied to that arousal
Making a facial expression can influence the corresponding emotional experience
Freud's pleasure-seeking, unconscious component of personality operating on the pleasure principle
Freud's rational mediator operating on the reality principle — mostly conscious
Freud's moral conscience — internalized values that produce pride or guilt
Defense mechanism: unconsciously blocking distressing thoughts from awareness
Defense mechanism: attributing one's own unacceptable feelings to others
Defense mechanism: behaving in the opposite manner from how one truly feels
Defense mechanism: channeling unacceptable impulses into socially valued activities
Freud's phallic stage conflict: unconscious desire for opposite-sex parent; resolved by identification
Bandura's view that personality emerges from mutual interaction of person, behavior, and environment
Bandura's term for belief in one's capacity to perform specific behaviors and achieve outcomes
Empirically derived personality traits: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism
Objective personality inventory with true/false items used to assess personality and detect psychopathology

exam_tip: The three emotion theories are tested frequently and are easy to confuse. Remember the sequence for James-Lange (body first, then emotion), simultaneous triggering for Cannon-Bard, and arousal + labeling for two-factor. For Freud, the AP exam focuses on the three structural components (id/ego/superego) and specific defense mechanisms — be ready to identify which defense mechanism is illustrated in a scenario (projection, rationalization, reaction formation, and sublimation appear most often). The Big Five traits are tested — remember OCEAN and know that conscientiousness is most predictive of success.
